analogy: Think of "Change and Development in Industrial Society" through the chapter's own opening images: Change and Development in Industrial Society 5 Social Change and Development in India 60 Bollywood in Mumbai, Maharashtra may be a place of dreams for you and me, but for many, it is their place of work. Like any industry, the workers there are part... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Change and Development in Industrial Society is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that change and Development in Industrial Society 5

The text explains that social Change and Development in India 60 Bollywood in Mumbai, Maharashtra may be a place of dreams for you and me, but for many, it is their place of work.

The text explains that like any industry, the workers there are part of unions.

The text explains that for instance, the dancers, stunt artists and the extras are all part of a junior artists association, whose demands include 8 hours shifts, proper wages and safe working conditions.

The text explains that the products of this industry are advertised and marketed through film distributors and cinema hall owners or through shops in the form of music cassettes and videos.

The text explains that and the people who work in this industry, as in any other, live in the same city, but depending on who they are and how much they earn, they do very different things in that city.

The text explains that film stars and textile mill owners live in places like Juhu, while extras and textile workers may live in places like Girangaon.

The text explains that some go to five star hotels and eat Japanese sushi and some eat vada pav from the local handcart.

The text explains that the residents of Mumbai are divided by where they live, what they eat and how much their clothes cost.

The text explains that but they are also united by certain common things that a city provides – they watch the same films and cricket matches, they suffer from the same air pollution and they all have aspirations for their children to do well.

The text explains that how and where people work and what kind of jobs they have is an important part of who they are.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will see how changes in technology or the kind of work that is available has changed social relations in India.

The text explains that on the other hand, social institutions like caste, kinship networks, gender and region also influence the way that work is organised or the way in which products are marketed.

The text explains that this is a major area of research for sociologists.

The text explains that for instance, why do we find more women in certain jobs like nursing or teaching than in other sectors like engineering?

The text explains that is this just a coincidence or is it because society thinks that women are suited for caring and nurturing work as against jobs which are seen as ‘tough’ and masculine?

The text explains that change and Development in Industrial Society 65 the mistri also put community-related pressures on the worker.

The text explains that the firm has outsourced all services like cleaning, and security, as well as the manufacture of parts.

The text explains that another reason is that overwork is built into the structure of outsourced projects: project costs and timelines are usually underestimated in terms of mandays, and because mandays are based on an eight-hour day, engineers have to put in extra hours and days in order to meet the deadlines.

The text explains that but even when there is no real work pressure, they tend to stay late in office either due to peer pressure or because they want to show the boss that they are working hard.

The text explains that from a nation of interfering joint families, the nature of work in a globalised economy is taking people in the direction of loneliness and vulnerability.

The text explains that 1999. ‘The Study of Industrial Labour in post-colonial India – The Formal Sector: An Introductory review’.

The text explains that 1999. ‘The Study of Industrial Labour in post-colonial India – The Informal Sector: A concluding review’.

The text explains that down and Out: Labouring Under Global Capitalism.

The text explains that in Illina Sen, A Space within the Struggle. pp.

The text explains that industry and Inequality: The Social Anthropology of Indian Labour.

The text explains that lost Worlds: Indian Labour and its Forgotten Histories Delhi.

The text explains that one Hundred Years, One Hundred Voices: the Millworkers of Girangaon: An Oral History.

The text explains that hard Drive: Working Conditions and Workers Struggles at Maruti.

The text explains that culture incorporated: control over work and workers in the Indian software outsourcing industry.

The text explains that presented at International Conference on New Global Workforces and Virtual Workplaces: Connections, Culture, and Control, National Institute of Advanced Studies, Bangalore.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India, Chapter 5: Change and Development in Industrial Society

prelims_traps: Do not treat 5.1 Images of IndustrIal socIety and 5.2 IndustrIalIsatIon In IndIa as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Change and Development in Industrial Society; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Change and Development in Industrial Society through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 5.1 Images of IndustrIal socIety and 5.2 IndustrIalIsatIon In IndIa to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Change and Development in Industrial Society, which statement is supported by the source text about 5.5 Working conditions?
options: Ask yourself why some coffee advertisements in India display two cups on the package whereas in America they show one cup?
The answer is that for many Indians drinking coffee is not an individual wake up activity, but an occasion to socialise with others.
Sociologists are interested in the questions of who produces what, who works where, who sells to whom and how.
These are not individual choices, but outcomes of social patterns.
